1.name: Easy
1.description: Factual questions directly from the text

2.name: Medium
2.description: Interpretive questions about meaning and themes

questions.question: What kind of animal offering does the LORD accept?

options.A: A male without blemish from the cattle or flock
options.B: Any animal, with or without blemish

questions.explanation: Leviticus 1:3 states that if the burnt offering be of the herd, the animal must be a male without blemish.

questions.question: What is a meat offering made of?

options.A: Fine flour, oil, and frankincense

questions.explanation: Leviticus 2:1 says 'his offering shall be of fine flour; and he shall pour oil upon it, and put frankincense thereon.'

questions.question: What is a sacrifice of peace offering?

options.A: A male or female animal without blemish from the herd

questions.explanation: Leviticus 3:1 says 'if his oblation be a sacrifice of peace offering... whether it be a male or female, he shall offer it without blemish.'

questions.question: What should the anointed priest bring for a sin offering?

options.A: A young bullock without blemish

questions.explanation: Leviticus 4:3 says 'let him bring for his sin, which he hath sinned, a young bullock without blemish unto the LORD.'

questions.question: What should a person do when they become guilty of a sin?

options.A: Confess the sin and bring a trespass offering

questions.explanation: Leviticus 5:5-6 says 'he shall confess that he hath sinned... and he shall bring his trespass offering unto the LORD.'

questions.question: How was Aaron prepared for the priesthood?

options.A: He was washed with water and clothed with priestly garments
options.B: He was anointed with oil only
options.C: He was crowned with a helmet

questions.explanation: Leviticus 8:6-7 says 'Moses brought Aaron and his sons, and washed them with water... he put upon him the coat and girdle.'

questions.question: What sign did God give to show His approval of Aaron's sacrifices?

options.A: Fire came out from before the LORD and consumed the altar

questions.explanation: Leviticus 9:24 says 'there came a fire out from before the LORD, and consumed upon the altar the burnt offering and the fat.'

questions.question: What happened to Nadab and Abihu?

options.A: Fire came out from the LORD and devoured them

questions.explanation: Leviticus 10:1-2 says they 'offered strange fire before the LORD... and there went out fire from the LORD, and devoured them.'

questions.question: What were the priests not to drink when entering the tabernacle?

options.A: Wine nor strong drink

questions.explanation: Leviticus 10:9 says 'Do not drink wine nor strong drink, thou, nor thy sons with thee, when ye go into the tabernacle.'

questions.question: What are the characteristics of clean animals according to Leviticus?

options.A: Those that part the hoof and chew the cud

questions.explanation: Leviticus 11:3 says 'Whatsoever parteth the hoof, and is clovenfooted, and cheweth the cud, among the beasts, that shall ye eat.'

questions.question: Why is the swine considered unclean?

options.A: Though it divides the hoof, it does not chew the cud

questions.explanation: Leviticus 11:7 says 'the swine, though he divide the hoof, and be clovenfooted, yet he cheweth not the cud; he is unclean.'

questions.question: What fish are considered clean to eat?

options.A: Those that have fins and scales

questions.explanation: Leviticus 11:9 says 'whatsoever hath fins and scales in the waters... them shall ye eat.'

questions.question: On what day was circumcision to be performed?

options.A: The eighth day

questions.explanation: Leviticus 12:3 says 'in the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised.'

questions.question: What did a priest look for to determine if someone had leprosy?

options.A: If the hair in the plague was turned white and the plague was deeper than the skin
options.C: If the person had a fever

questions.explanation: Leviticus 13:3 says 'when the hair in the plague is turned white, and the plague in sight be deeper than the skin of his flesh, it is a plague of leprosy.'

questions.question: Where were lepers required to dwell?

options.A: Without the camp

questions.explanation: Leviticus 13:46 says 'he shall dwell alone; without the camp shall his habitation be.'

questions.question: What happened when a leper was healed?

options.A: The priest would go out to examine him and perform a cleansing ritual
options.B: The leper automatically returned to the camp

questions.explanation: Leviticus 14:2-3 says 'This shall be the law of the leper... He shall be brought unto the priest... the priest shall look, and if the plague be healed.'

questions.question: What items were used in the cleansing of a leper?

options.A: Two birds alive and clean, cedar wood, scarlet, and hyssop

questions.explanation: Leviticus 14:4 says 'the priest command to take for him... two birds alive and clean, and cedar wood, and scarlet, and hyssop.'

questions.question: What made a person unclean according to Leviticus 15?

options.A: A running issue out of his flesh

questions.explanation: Leviticus 15:2 says 'When any man hath a running issue out of his flesh, because of his issue he is unclean.'

questions.question: What was done with the two goats on the Day of Atonement?

options.A: Aaron cast lots, one for the LORD and one for the scapegoat
options.C: Both were released into the wilderness
options.D: One was killed and one escaped

questions.explanation: Leviticus 16:8 says 'Aaron shall cast lots upon the two goats; one lot for the LORD, and the other lot for the scapegoat.'

questions.question: What did the scapegoat symbolize?

options.A: It bore all the iniquities of Israel away into the wilderness

questions.explanation: Leviticus 16:21-22 says Aaron would 'confess over him all the iniquities... putting them upon the head of the goat... the goat shall bear upon him all their iniquities.'

questions.question: On what day was the Day of Atonement celebrated?

options.A: The tenth day of the seventh month
options.B: The first day of the month

questions.explanation: Leviticus 16:29 says 'on the tenth day of the month, ye shall afflict your souls, and do no work.'

questions.question: Why was blood precious in God's sight?

options.A: The life of the flesh is in the blood and it makes atonement for souls
options.D: It was a symbol of power

questions.explanation: Leviticus 17:11 says 'For the life of the flesh is in the blood: and I have given it to you upon the altar to make an atonement for your souls.'

questions.question: What did God command Israel not to do?

options.A: Not to do after the doings of Egypt or Canaan, but keep God's judgments

questions.explanation: Leviticus 18:3-4 says 'After the doings of... Egypt... and Canaan... shall ye not do... Ye shall do my judgments.'

questions.question: What two things did God command Israel to do?

options.A: Fear father and mother, and keep the Sabbaths

questions.explanation: Leviticus 19:3 says 'Ye shall fear every man his mother, and his father, and keep my sabbaths.'

questions.question: What is the greatest commandment regarding neighbors?

options.A: Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself

questions.explanation: Leviticus 19:18 says 'thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself.'

questions.question: What did God command regarding the Sabbaths and sanctuary?

options.A: Keep the Sabbaths and reverence the sanctuary

questions.explanation: Leviticus 19:30 says 'Ye shall keep my sabbaths, and reverence my sanctuary.'

questions.question: What did God say He would do to Israel?

options.A: Sanctify them

questions.explanation: Leviticus 20:8 says 'I am the LORD which sanctify you.'

questions.question: For whom could priests become defiled by the dead?

options.A: For their near kin: mother, father, son, daughter, or sister

questions.explanation: Leviticus 21:2-3 lists the relations for whom priests could be defiled.

questions.question: What were priests required to be?

options.A: Holy unto their God

questions.explanation: Leviticus 21:6 says 'They shall be holy unto their God.'

questions.question: What did God command priests to do?

options.A: Separate themselves from holy things and not profane God's name
options.B: Keep all holy things for themselves

questions.explanation: Leviticus 22:2 says priests shall 'separate themselves from the holy things... and profane not my holy name.'

questions.question: What is the Sabbath day?

options.A: A day of rest, an holy convocation, no work to be done

questions.explanation: Leviticus 23:3 says 'the seventh day is the sabbath of rest, an holy convocation; ye shall do no work therein.'

questions.question: When is the LORD's Passover?

options.A: In the fourteenth day of the first month at even
options.B: In the fifteenth day of the first month

questions.explanation: Leviticus 23:5 says 'In the fourteenth day of the first month at even is the LORD's passover.'

questions.question: How long is the feast of unleavened bread?

options.A: Seven days

questions.explanation: Leviticus 23:6 says 'on the fifteenth day of the same month is the feast of unleavened bread unto the LORD: seven days ye must eat unleavened bread.'

questions.question: How many days are counted for Pentecost?

options.A: Fifty days from the wave offering

questions.explanation: Leviticus 23:15-16 says to count 'seven sabbaths... Even unto the morrow after the seventh sabbath shall ye number fifty days.'

questions.question: What festival occurs on the first day of the seventh month?

options.A: A memorial of blowing of trumpets

questions.explanation: Leviticus 23:24 says 'In the seventh month, in the first day of the month, shall ye have a sabbath, a memorial of blowing of trumpets.'

questions.question: What is the Feast of Tabernacles for?

options.A: For seven days unto the LORD in the seventh month

questions.explanation: Leviticus 23:34 says 'The fifteenth day of this seventh month shall be the feast of tabernacles for seven days unto the LORD.'

questions.question: What were Israelites to do during the Feast of Tabernacles?

options.A: Dwell in booths for seven days

questions.explanation: Leviticus 23:42 says 'Ye shall dwell in booths seven days.'

questions.question: What were the children of Israel to bring for the tabernacle lamps?

options.A: Pure oil olive beaten for the light

questions.explanation: Leviticus 24:2 says 'bring unto thee pure oil olive beaten for the light, to cause the lamps to burn continually.'

questions.question: How many cakes of showbread were to be set on the table?

options.A: Twelve cakes, six on each row

questions.explanation: Leviticus 24:6 says 'set them in two rows, six on a row, upon the pure table.'

questions.question: What was the punishment for blaspheming the name of the LORD?

options.A: Death by stoning

questions.explanation: Leviticus 24:16 says 'he that blasphemeth the name of the LORD, he shall surely be put to death... all the congregation shall certainly stone him.'

questions.question: What is the law of retaliation in Leviticus?

options.A: Eye for eye, tooth for tooth, as he hath caused, so shall it be done to him

questions.explanation: Leviticus 24:20 says 'eye for eye, tooth for tooth: as he hath caused a blemish... so shall it be done to him again.'

questions.question: What is the Sabbath year?

options.A: Every seventh year, a rest for the land

questions.explanation: Leviticus 25:2-3 describes a year of rest for the land in the seventh year when nothing is sown.

questions.question: What is the jubilee year?

options.A: Every fiftieth year, a holy year of rest and release

questions.explanation: Leviticus 25:10 describes the jubilee as occurring after seven sabbaths of years, making it every fiftieth year.

questions.question: What are the blessings for keeping God's statutes?

options.A: Rain in season, increase of the land, peace and safety

questions.explanation: Leviticus 26:3-10 describes multiple blessings for obedience including rainfall and agricultural abundance.

questions.question: What mixtures were forbidden in Israel?

options.A: Cattle with diverse kind, mingled seed in fields, and garments of linen and woolen mixed

questions.explanation: Leviticus 19:19 forbids various mixtures as part of maintaining separation and purity.

questions.question: What makes an unclean animal according to the law?

options.A: Those that do not meet the criteria of clean animals (partition hoof, chew cud, etc.)

questions.explanation: Leviticus 11 sets forth specific criteria for cleanliness and uncleanness of animals.

questions.question: What perpetual statute did God give regarding fat and blood?

options.A: Israel shall eat neither fat nor blood

questions.explanation: Leviticus 3:17 states this as a perpetual statute for their generations throughout all their dwellings.

questions.question: What does a burnt offering produce?

options.A: A sweet savour unto the LORD

questions.explanation: Leviticus 1:9 says the priest 'shall burn all on the altar... of a sweet savour unto the LORD.'

questions.question: What should the priests do regarding the fire on the altar?

options.A: The fire shall ever be burning; it shall never go out
options.B: The fire should be extinguished daily
options.C: The fire can be allowed to go out
options.D: Fire should be added only on the Sabbath

questions.explanation: Leviticus 6:13 states 'The fire shall ever be burning upon the altar; it shall never go out.'

questions.question: What core command did God give to all Israel?

options.A: Ye shall be holy: for I the LORD your God am holy

questions.explanation: Leviticus 19:2 contains the fundamental call to holiness that underlies all Levitical law.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the various offerings outlined in Leviticus?

options.A: They create a comprehensive system for maintaining relationship between God and Israel through prescribed sacrifices
options.B: They are merely food for the priests

questions.explanation: The offering system represents God's provision for Israel to approach Him, maintain covenant relationship, and deal with sin through divinely prescribed means.

questions.question: What does the distinction between offerings for intentional and unintentional sins teach?

options.A: God recognizes degrees of culpability and provides different remedies for different types of transgression

questions.explanation: The careful distinctions in Leviticus 4-7 show God's justice - He holds people accountable but also provides forgiveness graduated by the nature of the offense.

questions.question: What does the eternal burning fire on the altar symbolize?

options.A: God's perpetual presence and His constant willingness to receive offerings of atonement
options.B: A literal fire that provides warmth

questions.explanation: The perpetual fire represents God's ongoing availability for relationship and reconciliation - His covenant commitment to Israel doesn't diminish or depend on human performance.

questions.question: What is significant about the ordination of Aaron and the glory that appears?

options.A: God's approval of the priesthood comes through both the establishment of the priests and the visible sign of divine presence
options.C: God was displeased with the priests
options.D: The fire was a natural occurrence

questions.explanation: The pattern of proper ordination followed by visible divine manifestation shows that God sanctions this priesthood and accepts their mediation between Him and Israel.

questions.question: Why would God execute Nadab and Abihu for offering 'strange fire'?

options.A: It demonstrates that approaching God demands strict obedience to His revealed will; casual worship or unauthorized innovation is rebellion
options.D: Any innovation is permitted in worship

questions.explanation: The death of Nadab and Abihu establishes that holiness has standards - God cannot be approached by private invention or assumption. This teaches that true worship requires submission to God's design.

questions.question: Why would dietary laws be given to Israel?

options.A: They remind Israel daily of their covenant commitment to be separate and holy; constant distinctions between clean/unclean maintain consciousness of identity
options.B: They are purely for health reasons

questions.explanation: The dietary laws (kashrut) function as ongoing reminders that Israel is a holy people set apart. Every meal becomes an opportunity for covenant consciousness.

questions.question: What does the elaborate leprosy protocol reveal about God's concern?

options.A: God cares about both physical restoration and community reintegration; healing isn't complete until the healed person is restored to the congregation

questions.explanation: The fact that cleansing involves not just ritual but also examination, waiting, and ceremonial restoration shows God's holistic concern for the whole person and their place in community.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the detailed laws regarding uncleanness?

options.A: To keep Israel separate from uncleanness so they don't defile God's tabernacle; cleanness reflects covenant relationship
options.C: To give priests power over people

questions.explanation: The concern about defilement of the tabernacle shows these laws are about maintaining sacred space and covenant relationship, not mere hygiene.

questions.question: What does the Day of Atonement accomplish for Israel?

options.A: It provides annual corporate cleansing from all sin, restoring Israel's relationship with God and the tabernacle's sanctity
options.D: It occurs multiple times per year

questions.explanation: Yom Kippur represents God's gracious provision for systematic restoration of covenant relationship - no sin accumulates beyond what this day addresses.

questions.question: What theological concept does the scapegoat represent?

options.A: Vicarious bearing of sin - one bears the consequences on behalf of all, removing guilt from the community

questions.explanation: The scapegoat introduces the idea that sin can be transferred and removed, with one bearing what the many deserve - a proto-type of vicarious atonement.

questions.question: Why is the blood given authority for atonement?

options.A: Because the life is in the blood; blood represents the life given in place of the penalty deserved by the sinner

questions.explanation: This verse establishes that atonement works through life-for-life substitution - the animal's blood (life) satisfies the demand for the sinner's life.

questions.question: Why would God give strict sexual and relational laws to Israel?

options.A: To establish family stability and community cohesion, preventing the social chaos that characterized pagan societies

questions.explanation: The laws protect family structure, ensure clear lineage, prevent exploitation, and distinguish Israel from the pagan societies whose sexual chaos led to their judgment.

questions.question: What does the requirement to leave gleanings for the poor teach?

options.A: Provision for the poor is a religious obligation, not charity; God builds care for vulnerable into the economic system
options.D: Laws should not address economic issues

questions.explanation: This law makes care for the poor structural rather than optional - it's built into how harvests work, reflecting God's priority for justice within economic life.

questions.question: How radical is the command to 'love thy neighbour as thyself'?

options.A: It requires the same commitment to others' welfare that one has to one's own, an absolute standard of equal concern

questions.explanation: This is an extraordinary command - it requires viewing others' good with the same urgency and care one naturally has for oneself, making love the fundamental ethical principle.

questions.question: What does God's statement 'I have severed you from other people' mean for Israel?

options.A: Israel has been chosen and separated for distinctive covenant relationship and purpose distinct from other nations
options.B: Israel is superior to all people

questions.explanation: Separation is for covenant purpose and holiness, not for racial superiority or physical isolation - it means distinctive identity and calling.

questions.question: What makes priests holy in Leviticus?

options.A: Their consecration through God's action and their commitment to holy standards; they are sanctified through God's work, not earned by merit

questions.explanation: Holiness is not achieved but conferred - priests are sanctified by God, which then requires them to maintain standards appropriate to their status.

questions.question: Why would God institute multiple festivals throughout the year?

options.A: To create rhythm in Israel's life that constantly reminds them of God's acts, provisions, and their covenant relationship

questions.explanation: The festival calendar structures time itself around God's redemptive history - every season points to the Exodus or God's provision, making theology embodied in calendar.

questions.question: What is revolutionary about the Jubilee year?

options.A: It resets economic relationships every fifty years, preventing permanent class divisions and ensuring no family remains eternally enslaved or impoverished

questions.explanation: Jubilee is radical economic justice - it prevents inherited debt servitude and ensures resources return to original families, embodying God's concern for sustainable justice.

questions.question: What would Israel's obedience produce according to Leviticus 26?

options.A: Abundant provision, security, and the continuation of God's covenant presence manifest in tangible blessing
options.B: Only spiritual benefits with no material change

questions.explanation: The blessings are comprehensive - agricultural abundance, military security, and most importantly, God's continued presence and faithfulness to the covenant.

questions.question: What do the curses of Leviticus 26 reveal about God?

options.A: God is not indifferent to covenant violation; His justice means that rebellion has real consequences, but even these serve to call Israel back to covenant
options.C: God does not care about covenant

questions.explanation: The escalating curses show God takes His covenant seriously - consequences follow violation, but the very curses function as warnings to prompt repentance.

questions.question: Why are so many types of offerings required?

options.A: They provide comprehensive means for different situations: expressing devotion (burnt), acknowledging God's provision (thanksgiving), restoring relationship after sin (sin/trespass)

questions.explanation: The offering system is sophisticated - it addresses different spiritual situations and emotional states, showing that relationship with God is multifaceted.

questions.question: What is the primary role of the priest in Leviticus?

options.A: To teach Israel the distinction between holy and unholy, clean and unclean, maintaining their covenant consciousness

questions.explanation: Moses emphasizes teaching as central to priesthood - not just performing ritual, but helping Israel understand God's holiness standards.

questions.question: Why would the cleansing of a leper involve live and dead birds?

options.A: The dead bird represents judgment/death of the sickness; the living bird represents restoration/release, showing removal of condition

questions.explanation: The symbolism is powerful - what has held the person captive (represented by the dead bird's blood) is transferred; the live bird's release represents their freedom.

questions.question: What does 'eye for eye' justice accomplish?

options.A: It establishes proportional justice - punishment matches injury, preventing endless cycles of revenge escalation

questions.explanation: Lex talionis (eye for eye) is actually a limit on justice - it prevents revenge from becoming disproportionate, requiring measured response to injury.

questions.question: Why does Leviticus prohibit permanent slavery?

options.A: Because Israel themselves are God's servants, not ownable by other humans; permanent servitude would violate their covenant status

questions.explanation: Israel's fundamental identity is as God's servants - this status cannot be transferred to another human master. This protects human dignity rooted in covenant relationship.

questions.question: How does the offering system establish God's holiness and Israel's ability to approach?

options.A: The system demonstrates that approach to holiness requires substitution and mediation; sin cannot be casually dealt with but demands prescribed remedy

questions.explanation: The elaborate offering system teaches profound truths: God is holy (requiring proper approach), sin has consequences (requiring remedy), and restoration comes through divinely prescribed means, not human innovation.

questions.question: What theological trajectory do the events of Aaron's ordination to Nadab and Abihu's death establish?

options.A: That God's approval of ministry is conditional on submission to His will; the same God who validates also judges
options.B: That God only validates and never judges
options.C: That human institutions need no divine approval
options.D: That proximity to God guarantees blessing

questions.explanation: The progression (approval of proper ordination followed by judgment of presumption) establishes that being near God's presence without proper obedience is actually more dangerous, not safer.

questions.question: What unified principle underlies all the laws about clean and unclean, sickness and health?

options.A: That sacred distinction (holy/unholy, clean/unclean) is fundamental to existence; maintaining these distinctions honors God's creative order and maintains covenant consciousness
options.B: That all things are equally holy or unholy
options.D: That physical hygiene is the only concern

questions.explanation: These laws aren't primarily about health but about maintaining consciousness of holiness - they train the people to recognize and respect sacred boundaries in all of life.

questions.question: How does the Day of Atonement function in the covenant relationship?

options.A: It provides annual restoration after inevitable failure, preventing guilt from permanently severing covenant relationship; it embodies God's commitment to maintain relationship despite human sin
options.B: It eliminates the need for moral obedience
options.D: It is solely focused on ceremonial purification

questions.explanation: Yom Kippur reveals a central truth: God knows His people will sin, and He provides an annual mechanism for restoration. This is grace structuralized within law.

questions.question: What is the theological foundation for all of Levitical sacrifice?

options.A: That life-for-life substitution is the mechanism of atonement; because the life is in the blood, an innocent life can satisfy the justice-demand incurred by sin
options.B: That God is vindictive and requires suffering

questions.explanation: This verse provides the theological rationale for the entire sacrificial system - it explains how the death of an animal can accomplish anything. This concept will find its fulfillment in Christ.

questions.question: What does Leviticus' sexual ethics teach about God's purpose for human relationships?

options.A: Sexual relationships are sacred boundaries within the covenant; their violation damages both individuals and community; distinctions protect the vulnerable and establish stable social structures
options.D: All sexual relationships are equally acceptable

questions.explanation: These laws aren't prudish but protective - they prevent exploitation, establish clear lines of authority and responsibility, and create conditions where vulnerable people (particularly women and children) are protected.

questions.question: How does Leviticus 19 connect ritual holiness and moral holiness?

options.A: They are inseparable - being holy includes both proper worship (Sabbath, sanctuary reverence) and right conduct (honesty, justice, love), showing holiness pervades all of life
options.B: Ritual and morality are separate concerns

questions.explanation: Chapter 19 is revolutionary in placing commands about honest weights alongside commands about sacred days - holiness isn't compartmentalized into religious and secular spheres.

questions.question: What does the command about strangers reveal about God's covenant with Israel?

options.A: Israel's own experience of being strangers in Egypt establishes empathy as a covenant obligation; their past vulnerability becomes grounds for current compassion

questions.explanation: Israel is told: 'Remember you were slaves - therefore love the stranger.' This makes empathy covenantally rooted rather than optional; memory of deliverance demands compassion.

questions.question: Why would the priesthood be subject to stricter standards than the rest of Israel?

options.A: Proximity to holiness requires greater purity; priests mediate between holy God and people, so their fitness for this role demands higher standards

questions.explanation: The priest's role as mediator means they bear special responsibility - they cannot bring what is defiled into God's presence. Their standards reflect their unique calling.

questions.question: How does Israel's festival calendar function as theological education?

options.A: Each festival embodies and rehearses key covenant truths - Passover (liberation), Pentecost (God's giving of covenant), Tabernacles (God's protective presence), making history and theology lived experience
options.D: Each festival is independent of others

questions.explanation: The festival system is a sophisticated theological curriculum - the calendar itself teaches Israel's story repeatedly. Believers don't just remember history; they embody it annually.

questions.question: What economic theology does the Jubilee system embody?

options.A: That ultimate ownership belongs to God (the land cannot be permanently sold), and that human systems must periodically reset to prevent permanent inequality
options.B: That wealth should accumulate without limit
options.C: That economic systems need no justice principles
options.D: That God is unconcerned with economics

questions.explanation: Jubilee embodies radical economic theology: God owns the land, humans are stewards; permanent debt servitude violates God's ownership; justice requires periodic restoration of economic opportunity.

questions.question: How does Leviticus present the relationship between obedience and blessing?

options.A: Obedience is not transactional bargaining but reflects covenant reality - those who align with God's will experience the natural consequence of His blessing

questions.explanation: Leviticus presents a coherent world where covenant obedience aligns with God's purposes and naturally produces blessing, while violation produces fracture and curse - not because God is vindictive but because reality is structured this way.

questions.question: What is the overarching theme connecting all Levitical law?

options.A: That holiness is both God's nature and Israel's calling; maintaining the distinction between holy/unholy, clean/unclean throughout all of life makes Israel a holy people and preserves their covenant relationship
options.C: That ritual performance is the only concern

questions.explanation: Leviticus constantly returns to: 'I am the LORD your God... you shall be holy, for I am holy.' This verse frames all the specific laws - they are not burdensome restrictions but the shape of covenant relationship.

questions.question: What do the restrictions on leaven and requirement for salt in offerings reveal?

options.A: Leaven (which ferments/corrupts) represents corruption that must be excluded from offerings; salt (which preserves) represents covenant permanence; details matter in approach to holy God

questions.explanation: These specific details are not arbitrary - they teach that purity is required, covenant is permanent. Every element of an offering communicates theological truth.

questions.question: What does the inclusion of sins of omission alongside sins of commission teach?

options.A: Holiness encompasses both what we do and what we fail to do; not speaking up when witnessing wrong-doing is itself sin requiring atonement
options.D: Passive participation in wrong is acceptable

questions.explanation: By including witness-bearing failures and various unintentional violations, Leviticus shows that sin involves responsibility for the community, not just personal transgression - a revolutionary concept.
